humanities_role_explanation: Uses a curated corpus of art historical images paired with expert bilingual critiques (content) and designs a five-level evaluation framework specifically focused on measuring the depth of cultural, historical, and aesthetic interpretation in vision-language models (domain).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Multi-modal evaluation combining artwork images with bilingual (Chinese and English) textual prompts that require models to generate structured art critiques.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates open-ended critiques across five analytical levels: sensory recognition of forms and colors (L1), technical analysis of artistic medium and brushstrokes (L2), decoding of cultural symbolism (L3), attribution of historical and biographical context (L4), and interpretation of philosophical and aesthetic concepts (L5).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: No, it is a highly specialized, domain-specific benchmark focusing on cross-cultural art history and critique.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated 15 general-purpose vision-language models including GPT-4o, GPT-5, GPT-5.2, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Claude-Opus-4.5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen3-VL-235B, and Llama4-Scout.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Yes, generative VLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Visual Arts and Performing Arts (covers six cultural traditions: Western, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Islamic, and Indian art history with 165 culture-specific dimensions).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses automated quality indicators (DCR, CSA, CDS, LQS), single-judge rubric scoring, and sigmoid-calibrated scores validated against human expert ratings using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC), and Cohen's d.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the evaluated prompts/critiques are bilingual in English and Chinese.

summary: The paper introduces VULCA-BENCH, a tri-tier evaluation framework for assessing the cross-cultural art critique capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) across six global art traditions. It maps artistic understanding onto a theoretical five-level hierarchy (from basic visual description to deep philosophical interpretation) and applies sigmoid calibration to align LLM-judge scores with human expert evaluations. Testing on 15 state-of-the-art VLMs reveals systematic performance degradation at deeper interpretive levels and a significant, persistent bias favoring Western art.
key_findings: Gemini-2.5-Pro (4.27) and Qwen3-VL-235B (4.21) achieved the highest calibrated scores, while models generally degraded in performance when moving from surface visual description (L1-L2) to cultural interpretation (L3-L5). Critically, 13 of the 15 evaluated models scored Western art significantly higher than non-Western art (overall China-West gap Cohen's d = -0.74, p < 0.001).
paper_name: Cross-Cultural Expert-Level Art Critique Evaluation with Vision-Language Models
